cases.description: Positive fractional number truncates its decimal part (same result as INT here)
Documented classic divergence from INT: TRUNC simply removes the fractional part with no rounding, i.e. truncates TOWARD zero
cases.expected_note: Per Microsoft's TRUNC function docs, TRUNC(-8.9) = -8 (fractional part discarded, toward zero), whereas INT(-8.9) = -9 (rounds down, away from zero) -- see the paired INT_negative_rounds_toward_negative_infinity case in INT.json

cases.description: num_digits=2 keeps two decimal places, discarding the rest without rounding
cases.expected_note: 3.14159 truncated (not rounded) at 2 decimals is 3.14; if it were ROUND instead, 3.14159 would still round to 3.14 here, but TRUNC(3.145,2) vs ROUND(3.145,2) would diverge -- this case isolates the num_digits mechanic, not the rounding-vs-truncating distinction

cases.description: Negative num_digits truncates digits to the LEFT of the decimal point
cases.expected_note: Negative num_digits zeroes out the last 2 digits of the integer part without any rounding
